response.evidence: Analyzing Hispanic or Latino applicants across all race and sex categories: acceptance rates range from 77.8% to 94.0%, with most groups above 83%. Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants show comparable ranges of 85.9% to 94.9%. The differences are not systematically disadvantaging Hispanic or Latino applicants - in fact, several Hispanic or Latino groups show acceptance rates above 90% (e.g., Asian Female Hispanic 94.0%, American Indian Female Hispanic 91.1%, Asian Male Hispanic 92.2%). White Male Hispanic shows 90.5% vs White Male Non-Hispanic 93.6% (3.1 percentage point difference), which is modest. The data does not show clear systematic discrimination against Hispanic or Latino applicants in loan approval rates.

Analyzing Hispanic or Latino applicants across all race and sex categories: acceptance rates range from 78.9% to 94.0%, with most groups between 83-92%. Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants show similar ranges (85.9% to 94.9%). The average acceptance rate for Hispanic or Latino applicants is approximately 88.4%, compared to 90.7% for Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants - a difference of 2.3 percentage points. While this shows slightly lower acceptance rates for Hispanic/Latino applicants, the difference is modest and could be influenced by other financial factors in the data (loan amounts, debt-to-income ratios, property values) rather than discrimination alone. Several Hispanic/Latino groups actually show very high acceptance rates (e.g., Asian Female Hispanic: 94%, American Indian Female Hispanic: 91.1%).

The data shows acceptance rates for Hispanic or Latino applicants range from 78.9% to 94.0% across different race and sex groups. When compared to Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants in the same race/sex categories, Hispanic individuals often have similar or even higher acceptance rates. For example: White Male Hispanic (90.5%) vs White Male Non-Hispanic (93.6%), Asian Female Hispanic (94.0%) vs Asian Female Non-Hispanic (93.1%), and Black Female Hispanic (90.4%) vs Black Female Non-Hispanic (86.5%). The variations appear to be driven more by race and sex combinations than by Hispanic ethnicity alone. Overall acceptance rates for Hispanic applicants are competitive with non-Hispanic groups, with no systematic pattern of lower approval rates based on Hispanic/Latino ethnicity.

The data shows acceptance rates for Hispanic or Latino applicants ranging from 78.9% to 94.0% across different race and sex combinations. These rates are generally comparable to or higher than Non-Hispanic or Latino rates within the same demographic groups. For example: White Male Hispanic (90.5%) vs White Male Non-Hispanic (93.6%), Asian Male Hispanic (92.2%) vs Asian Male Non-Hispanic (93.1%), and White Female Hispanic (89.9%) vs White Female Non-Hispanic (93.5%). While some Hispanic groups show slightly lower acceptance rates, the differences are modest (typically 1-4 percentage points) and do not demonstrate a clear pattern of systematic discrimination. The data does not include age as a variable, so no analysis of age-based discrimination is possible.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants have acceptance rates comparable to or higher than non-Hispanic applicants across most race/sex categories. For example: White Male Hispanic (90.5%) vs White Male non-Hispanic (93.6%), Asian Male Hispanic (92.2%) vs Asian Male non-Hispanic (93.1%), White Female Hispanic (89.9%) vs White Female non-Hispanic (93.5%). The differences are relatively small (typically 1-4 percentage points) and non-Hispanic applicants sometimes have slightly higher rates. However, this pattern is inconsistent across groups, and some Hispanic groups show higher acceptance rates than their non-Hispanic counterparts (e.g., American Indian Female Hispanic 91.1% vs non-Hispanic 94.2%). The data does not show systematic evidence of discrimination against Hispanic or Latino applicants, though slight variations exist that could reflect other unmeasured factors like creditworthiness or loan characteristics.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants have acceptance rates ranging from 78.9% to 94.0% across different race and sex combinations, which fall within the overall range of acceptance rates (77.8% to 94.9%) for all demographic groups. While some variations exist, Hispanic or Latino groups do not consistently show lower acceptance rates compared to Non-Hispanic or Latino groups. For example: White Male Hispanic (90.5%) vs White Male Non-Hispanic (93.6%), Asian Male Hispanic (92.2%) vs Asian Male Non-Hispanic (93.1%), and White Female Hispanic (89.9%) vs White Female Non-Hispanic (93.5%). The differences are modest and Hispanic groups sometimes perform comparably or even better than their non-Hispanic counterparts in certain race/sex categories.

Hispanic or Latino applicants show acceptance rates ranging from 78.9% to 94.0% across different race and sex categories. These rates are comparable to or higher than Non-Hispanic or Latino applicants in most demographic groups. For example: White Male Hispanic (90.5%) vs White Male Non-Hispanic (93.6%), Asian Male Hispanic (92.2%) vs Asian Male Non-Hispanic (93.1%), and White Female Hispanic (89.9%) vs White Female Non-Hispanic (93.5%). While some Non-Hispanic groups show slightly higher acceptance rates (1-4 percentage points), the differences are relatively modest and Hispanic applicants consistently achieve acceptance rates above 78%, indicating general access to loan approval. The data does not provide strong evidence of systematic discrimination against Hispanic or Latino applicants, though slight disparities exist in some demographic categories.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants have acceptance rates ranging from 78.9% to 94.0% across different race and sex groups. These rates are comparable to or higher than non-Hispanic counterparts in most categories. For example: White Male Hispanic (90.5%) vs White Male non-Hispanic (93.6%), Asian Male Hispanic (92.2%) vs Asian Male non-Hispanic (93.1%), and White Female Hispanic (89.9%) vs White Female non-Hispanic (93.5%). While some non-Hispanic groups show slightly higher acceptance rates (1-4 percentage points in most cases), the differences are relatively modest and Hispanic applicants maintain high acceptance rates overall. The data does not show systematic evidence of discrimination based on Hispanic or Latino ethnicity alone.

Hispanic or Latino applicants show acceptance rates ranging from 78.9% to 94.0% across different race and sex categories. These rates are comparable to or exceed Non-Hispanic rates in most groups. For example: White Hispanic Males have 90.5% acceptance vs 93.6% for Non-Hispanic; Asian Hispanic Males 92.2% vs 93.1%; Black Hispanic Males 88.9% vs 85.9%. The data shows no systematic pattern of lower acceptance rates for Hispanic or Latino applicants. In several categories, Hispanic applicants have higher acceptance rates than their Non-Hispanic counterparts.

The data shows Hispanic or Latino applicants across all racial and sex categories have acceptance rates ranging from 78.9% to 94.0%, which are comparable to or higher than Non-Hispanic/Latino rates in the same demographic groups. For example: White Male Hispanic (90.5%) vs White Male Non-Hispanic (93.6%), Asian Male Hispanic (92.2%) vs Asian Male Non-Hispanic (93.1%), Black Male Hispanic (88.9%) vs Black Male Non-Hispanic (85.9%). The differences are small and bidirectional, with Hispanic applicants sometimes having higher and sometimes lower acceptance rates than their non-Hispanic counterparts within the same race-sex category. There is no consistent pattern of lower acceptance rates for Hispanic or Latino applicants that would indicate systematic discrimination based on ethnicity alone.
